segments.s360-g65: به ایران شویم از پس کار اوی — The clause opens with the preposition به + Iran (به ایران = 'into/to Iran'). شویم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/jussive (hortatory) form, here giving an exhortation 'let us go/let us be (in)'. از پس is a fixed prepositional sequence meaning 'after' or 'in pursuit of', and کار اوی is 'his affair/deed' with the enclitic اوی marking possession; the whole phrase means 'let us go into Iran to pursue his affairs'.
segments.s360-g66: نترسیم از آزار و پیکار اوی — نترسیم is the negative 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative form 'let us not fear' (or a resolved 'we will not fear'). از introduces the objects of fear; آزار و پیکار are coordinated nouns 'harm/injury and battle/fighting', and اوی is the possessive enclitic 'his', giving 'his harm and his fighting'.
segments.s360-g67: برانیمش از پیش و خوارش کنیم — برانیمش is a 1st-person-plural hortative/imperative-like form with the object enclitic -ش 'him', meaning 'let us drive him off/away'. از پیش here means 'from (our) presence' or 'from before us'. خوارش کنیم combines the adjective/root خوار 'despised/worthless' with the causative/do verb کنیم (we cause/make), again with -ش as 'him', so 'let us make him despised/abase him'.
segments.s360-g68: ببندیم و زنده به دارش کنیم — ببندیم is 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive of بندن with the intensive/presentive prefix ب- 'let us bind/tie'. The coordination links to the following clause: زنده (alive) plus the prepositional phrase به دارش ('to his/that gallows' with the enclitic -ش) and کنیم ('let us make/cause') yields the idiomatic command 'let us hang him alive'. Note the enclitic -ش attaches to دار here marking the person ('his' or 'him').
segments.s360-g57: مر here is an archaic emphatic/dative particle meaning 'for me' or 'on my behalf', placed before the clause. او را is the direct object with را marking 'him'. بگویی is a present/subjunctive/imperative form 'tell' (classical second- or jussive-style), introducing what follows as reported speech. کزین ('کز' + 'این') means 'from this' and governs راه زشت 'this ugly/vile way' as the thing to be abandoned.
segments.s360-g58: بگرد is an imperative from گشتن here meaning 'turn (away)' or 'go away'; و simply connects the two imperatives. بترس is an imperative 'fear' (be afraid of), followed by از with the object خدای بهشت 'the God of Paradise' (lit. 'God of Paradise'), so the construction means 'fear the God of Paradise' or 'be afraid of the God of Paradise'.
segments.s360-g59: مر again is the dative/benefactive 'for me' placed before the command. آن پیر ناپاک را is the object 'that impure old man' with را marking it. دور کن is the imperative 'make distant / put away' (i.e. 'drive away' or 'remove him').
segments.s360-g60: بر آیین ما means 'according to our custom' where بر = 'upon/on' and آیین = 'rite/custom'. The following بر is a preposition 'to/for' introducing the beneficiary/goal of the action; یکی سور کن is an imperative 'make one feast' (یکی = one/a single, سور = banquet, کن = do/make). So the whole phrase commands holding one banquet in accordance with their custom.
segments.s360-g61: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic adverbial particle meaning roughly 'now, at this time' (common in classical Persian). نپذیرد is the negative form 'does not accept / will not accept' (subjunctive/indicative sense depending on context). از ما سخن places سخن 'speech/talk' with the preposition از indicating the source/subject relation ('speak with/of us' or 'accept speech from us').
segments.s360-g62: کند is the present/subjunctive/optative 'let him do / he makes'. روی تازه is a noun + adjective 'a fresh/new face' (روی = 'face; countenance'), so کند روی تازه = 'he puts on/ shows a new face'. بما 'to us' is the indirect object. بر کهن literally 'upon the old' (بر = 'on/toward', کهن = 'old/ancient'); together the phrase contrasts the 'fresh face' with the former 'old' one (i.e. 'instead of the old').
segments.s360-g63: سپاه پراگنده is a noun phrase 'the scattered army' (سپاه = army, پراگنده = scattered). باز آوریم is a first-person-plural present/subjunctive/jussive 'let us bring back / we will bring back' (باز = again; آوریم = 'we bring' or hortatory 'let us bring'). The clause is an exhortation to reassemble the dispersed forces.
segments.s360-g64: یکی خوب لشکر is 'one good army/host' (یکی = one/a single, خوب = good/strong, لشکر = army). فراز آوریم is a compound meaning 'bring up / raise up' (فراز 'up/high' + آوریم 'let us bring'), so the second clause continues the exhortation: 'let us raise up/assemble a single excellent host.'
segments.s360-g41: A nominal string in apposition: 'سرنامداران ایران' = 'the leading men of Iran' followed by 'سپاه' = 'the host/army' used as a parallel title. Classical Persian often lists nouns without linking particles; here the nouns stand side by side as appositive epithets of the same person.
segments.s360-g42: 'گرانمایه' is an attributive adjective 'esteemed/valuable' modifying the following noun phrase 'فرزند لهراسپ شاه' = 'the son of Lohrasp the king.' Word order is adjective + noun + genitive (proper name + title); no ezāfe is written but the relation is clear from context.
segments.s360-g43: 'که' introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). 'گشتاسپ' is the name used, and 'خوانندش' is the verbal form 'they call him' (خوانند = 3rd pl. present subjunctive/optative used here as simple present; the clitic -ش = 'him'). 'ایرانیان' is the subject placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s360-g44: ببست is the past of بستن ('to bind/close/fasten') used transitively ('they/ he bound/placed'). 'او' is the object pronoun 'him'; 'یکی کشتی' = 'one/single ship' (یکی functioning as numeral/indefinite); 'بر میان' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the middle / amid' ('بر' + 'میان'). Word order here places the verb before its object pronoun and the following noun phrase.
segments.s360-g45: 'برادرش' = 'his brother' (برادر + possessive suffix -ش). 'نیز' = 'also/too'. 'آن سوار دلیر' is a noun phrase 'that brave rider/horseman' (آن = demonstrative, سوار = rider, دلیر = adjective 'brave') placed in apposition; classical epic style lists these epithets without a copula.
segments.s360-g46: 'سپهدار ایران' = 'the commander (سپهدار) of Iran' (title + place). 'که' introduces a relative clause 'whose...' and 'نامش' = 'his name' (نام + possessive -ش). 'زریر' is the proper name serving as the predicate of the relative clause. The relative clause thus reads 'whose name was Zarir.'
segments.s360-g47: 'همه' = 'all/everyone' (subject). 'پیش' is a preposition meaning 'before / in the presence of'. 'آن دین‌پژوه' = 'that religious scholar/theologian' (demonstrative + compound noun). 'آمدند' is the 3rd person plural past 'they came.' The clause places the prepositional phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s360-g48: 'ازان' = 'from that' (referring back to what follows). 'پیر جادو' is a nominal phrase 'that old magician' or 'old wizard' (پیر = old/elder; جادو = magic/magician in context). 'ستوه آمدند' is an idiomatic predicate: 'they came/stood/arrived weary' (ستوه = 'weary/put to suffering', آمدند = 'they came'), i.e. 'they arrived, worn out by the magician.'
segments.s360-g33: همی is a classical/progressive adverb meaning 'continually/keeps on'; گوید is the 3rd-person present archaic form 'he says' that introduces reported speech; آمدم is a first-person past inside that reported speech ('I came'), so the verb sequence shows a reporting verb (گوید) followed by the quoted clause (آمدم).
segments.s360-g34: ز نزد is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the presence of' (ز = from, نزد = near/in the presence of); خدای جهان is a genitive/compound 'God (or Lord) of the world'; آمدم is the quoted first‑person past verb completing the claimant's statement.
segments.s360-g35: خداوند را دیدم is a canonical Persian past clause with the definite object marked by را before the verb ('I saw the Lord'); اندر بهشت is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in Paradise' placed after the verb.
segments.s360-g36: من is the first‑person subject 'I'; این زند و استا is the object phrase listing religious texts (زَند = Zand commentary, استا here represents اوستا/Avesta); همه زو نوشت is the verb phrase 'wrote them all down' with همه 'all' and نوشت past 'wrote'. (زو in classical usage functions as an oblique pronoun/particle; the line is compressed in poetic diction.)
segments.s360-g37: بدوزخ is a fused prepositional/adverbial phrase ('in/into Hell'); درون reinforces 'inside/within'; دیدم is the first‑person past 'I saw'; آهرمنا names the object (Ahriman) — word order here places the locative phrase before the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s360-g38: نیارستمش is a negative past form with the enclitic object -ش ('him') — literally 'I did not (or could not) help/hold/withstand him' (classical/archaic sense); گشت is a past of گشتن 'turned/became/moved' and پیرامنا is an archaic form meaning 'around/about (us/it)'; together they describe that he went about/turned around.
segments.s360-g39: گروگر (a missionary/partisan/agent) appears as the (direct) object before فرستادم 'I sent'—a normal Persian past transitive clause; از بهر دین is a purpose phrase 'for the sake of the faith/religion' (از بهر = 'for').
segments.s360-g40: گفتا is the past reporting verb 'he said'; بیارای here is an imperative/infinitival/archaic verb form meaning roughly 'present/arrange/bring' (the provided sense list didn't include this verbal sense); به دانش زمین is the indirect object/goal 'to the learning/learned people of the land' (دانش used in the sense of learning/the learned, and زمین 'land/country').
segments.s360-g9: This clause is a negative past-tense statement with the indirect object first: به ترکان (to the Turks) + نداد (did not give). ایچ کس is an emphatic negative quantifier (literally “no/none person”) meaning “no one.” باژ و ساو are two coordinated nouns following the verb as the (accusative) objects; both are archaic/poetic items whose exact referent is context-dependent (see senses). Word order here (to‑object — verb — negative quantifier + subject) reflects older epic Persian syntax and topical fronting.
segments.s360-g10: This short prepositional/time phrase modifies the whole clause: برین روزگار گذشته = 'in this/these past days' (بر + این with poetic orthography) where برین is the fused form meaning 'in/at this', روزگار گذشته = 'the past time(s)/those days'. بتاو is a dative/enclitic-like form 'to him/unto that' pointing the reference back to a previously mentioned person or to the matter just discussed.
segments.s360-g11: پذیرفت is the simple past 'accepted' (he accepted). گشتاسپ is the subject/name. گفتا is the archaic past form 'said' (classical/poetic past). که introduces a reported clause ('that') and نیز (= also/too) links Gushtasp's remark to what preceded.
segments.s360-g12: نفرمایمش is a single literary form: نفرمایم (negative of فرماییدن, 'to command/say') with the object clitic -ش ('it/him'), so here 'I will not order it' or 'I will not tell/command him.' It governs the following infinitive دادن ('to give'), so the idiom means 'I will not order the giving of ...'. این = 'this' qualifies باژ, and چیز ('thing') is a generic noun making 'این باژ چیز' = 'this baž-thing' (a fixed, somewhat indefinite phrase).
segments.s360-g13: پس = 'then'. آگاه شد is a compound past: آگاه (aware) + شد (became) → 'became aware'. نره دیوی is a nominal phrase 'the male demon' (نره = masculine/male; دیوی = demon). ازین = 'of this' (the preposition از + این) marks what he became aware of.
segments.s360-g14: هم‌اندرز زمان = 'this joint/advising admonition' or 'at that time' (هم‌اندرز = counsel/admonition; زمان adds temporal force). شد here again = 'happened/became' and functions to link to the following motion: سوی = 'toward' and شاه چین = 'the Chinese king' (شاه + چین). Overall this segment states that, on that counsel/time, he turned toward the Chinese ruler.
segments.s360-g15: بدو = 'to him/unto him' (dative clitic-like prepositional form). گفت here is the verb 'said' (classical narrative past). کای is the vocative particle 'O' used in epic address (kāy/kāyān), and شهریار جهان = 'sovereign/king of the world' is the vocative title addressed to the ruler.
segments.s360-g16: This is the content of the address: جهان یکسره پیش تو = 'the whole world entirely before you' (جهان 'world' + یکسره 'completely; wholly' + پیش 'before/in the presence of' + تو 'you'). چون کهان is a poetic phrase meaning 'as in former/ancient times' (چون = 'as', کهان = 'the old/ancient' or 'the former world'), giving the sense 'as in olden days.'
segments.s360-g1: This is a temporal clause introduced by چو ('when/after'). چندی is a duration 'for a while/after some time', برآمد is the past-tense verb 'arose/appeared', and برین روزگار is an adverbial phrase 'in that time/age' (بر = on/in, ین = the demonstrative form of این, روزگار = time/age). Together the segment means 'when/after some time in that age'.
segments.s360-g2: خجسته is an adjective 'auspicious/blessed', ببود is an archaic past copula 'was', and اختر شهریار is a genitive phrase 'star of the sovereign' (اختر = star, شهریار = king/ruler). Word order is adjectival — verb — noun phrase: 'an auspicious star was (for) the sovereign'.
segments.s360-g3: به marks the indirect object 'to', شاه کیان is the title 'the Kian (Kayanian) king' (شاه + کیان as dynasty), گفت is the verb 'said' (here used as past tense), and زردشت پیر is the subject 'Zoroaster the elder'. The order (to X said Y) is a standard Persian poetic/news order where the indirect object comes before the verb and subject.
segments.s360-g4: که introduces reported speech ('that'); در دین ما is 'in our religion' (در = in, دین = religion, ما = our), این is the demonstrative 'this' referring to the person Hojir, and نباشد is a negative subjunctive/present form meaning 'should not be / must not be' (3rd singular negative). The whole clause says 'that in our religion this (Hojir) should not be'.
segments.s360-g5: که introduces the content of the complaint ('that'); تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you'; باژ بدهی is a verbal phrase where بدهی (subjunctive/present) means 'you give' and باژ (here) means 'tribute/levy' (poetic/older vocabulary), and به سالار چین marks the indirect object 'to the commander of China'. The clause reports 'that you give tribute to the Chinese commander.'
segments.s360-g6: نه is a negation/emphatic 'no' (here rejecting the proposal). اندر خور دین ما باشد این is an idiomatic, somewhat inverted poetic phrase: اندر = 'within/in', خور here functions in the idiom 'اندر خورِ ... بودن' meaning 'to be fitting/part of ...' (the lexeme has specialized meaning in this expression), دین ما = 'our religion', باشد is the subjunctive/copula 'should be/is fitting', and این = 'this'. Together: 'no — this should not be part of our religion.' Note the poetically inverted word order (verb before the demonstrative).
segments.s360-g7: نباشم is a first‑person negative form meaning 'I will not be / I am not willing to be' (archaic/poetic subjunctive/optative use). برین (بر + ین) = 'with this/over this' (the demonstrative), نیز = 'also', and همداستان is 'like‑minded / in agreement' (here 'being complicit or of the same mind'). The phrase expresses refusal: 'I will not be likewise of this mind.'
segments.s360-g8: که here introduces the reason/explanation 'for/because': شاهان ما درگه باستان = 'our kings (شاهان ما) (are) of the ancient court' (درگه = royal court, باستان = ancient). The copula is omitted (typical of poetry), so this clause explains why the speaker refuses to agree.
segments.s360-g25: از اندوه او is a causal/ablative phrase ('because of/from his grief'); او is the possessor of اندوه. Two adjectives سست و بیمار are coordinated by و and describe the (unstated) person; شد is the past-tense copular verb 'became'. The whole clause therefore means 'he became weak and sick from his grief.'
segments.s360-g26: دل و جان او is a coordinated nominal phrase 'his heart and soul' (او follows to mark possession of both nouns). پر is an adjective 'full (of)'; it takes ز + noun to mark what it is full of. تیمار is the object of ز ('care/worry'); شد is the past 'became'. So 'his heart and soul became full of care.'
segments.s360-g27: In poetic word order the noun phrase تگینان لشکرش را functions as the object 'the commanders of his army' (لشکرش + را marks definiteness/object); پیش خواند is a compound idea 'called forward / summoned before (him/there)'. خواند is past 'called/summoned'.
segments.s360-g28: شنیده modifies سخن: here شنیده is adjectival 'well-known / heard (i.e. famed)' and سخن = 'speech'. پیش ایشان is a locative phrase 'before them / in their presence'. براند is an archaic past-form meaning 'he placed/put forward / uttered' (literally 'brought/put'), so the clause says he presented his famed speech before them.
segments.s360-g29: بدانید is an imperative 'know / understand' (addressing the audience). گفتا is the archaic past 'he said' placed after the imperative. کز (= که + از) introduces the subordinate content clause and here functions roughly as 'that/from', leading into the source phrase ایران‌زمین 'the land of Iran'.
segments.s360-g30: بشد is an older past/preterite form 'it came to be / it happened / arose'. What arose are the coordinated nouns فره و دانش و پاک دین: فره = divine glory/honor, دانش = knowledge/learning, پاک دین = 'pure religion' (پاک qualifies دین). The verb precedes the list (verb-first order common in epic style).
segments.s360-g31: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing someone). جادو here functions as the agental noun '(one versed in) magic' — 'a magician/sorcerer' in archaic usage. آمد is simple past 'came'. به دین آوری is a purposive phrase: به + noun/verb-noun expresses purpose 'to bring/introduce a religion'; آوری is from آوردن 'to bring'.
segments.s360-g32: به ایران is the directional 'to/into Iran'. The second به begins a phrase of manner or pretense: به دعوی پیغمبری = 'with the claim/pretension of prophethood' (دعوی = claim; پیغمبری = prophethood). In other words, he came to Iran to introduce a religion while claiming to be a prophet.
segments.s360-g17: به‌جای آوردند (here a compound verbal expression) = 'they fulfilled / carried out'. The phrase puts the light verb آوریدند (past, 3rd-person plural) after the locative-like particle به جای to form the idiom 'به‌جای آوردن' = 'to accomplish/perform'. فرمان تو is the direct object (فرمان 'command' followed by the possessive pronoun تو 'your').
segments.s360-g18: نتابد is the negative present/potential form of تابیدن (here sense 'turn/bend'), meaning 'does not/shall not turn (away)' — used idiomatically to mean 'not deviate' or 'not break'. کسی with a negative verb yields a universal negative ('no one'). سر ز پیمان is an idiom: سر ز (lit. 'head from') + پیمان 'covenant/pledge' = 'to break/depart from the pledge'. تو again marks the possessor 'your'.
segments.s360-g19: مگر here functions as an exception/conditional marker 'except / but'. پورلهراسپ گشتاسپ شاه is a name + title chain: پورلهراسپ 'son of Lohrasp' (a patronymic), گشتاسپ (the personal name), شاه 'king' (postpositive title). Classical Persian often strings name elements in this order.
segments.s360-g20: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that'. آرد is the present/habitual narrative form of آوردن 'to bring' (3rd-person singular) — here 'brings/would bring'. همی is the archaic/prosodic continuous/emphatic marker indicating ongoing or habitual action. سوی ترکان سپاه literally orders as 'toward the Turks (سوی ترکان) the army (سپاه)' — note the object noun سپاه appears after the prepositional phrase for poetic word order: 'who brings an army toward the Turks.'
segments.s360-g21: برد here functions as a past verbal sense 'bore / displayed' (classical usage of بردن); آشکارا is an adverb 'openly / plainly'; همه دشمنی = 'all enmity' (همه 'all' modifying دشمنی 'enmity/hostility'). The clause means 'he openly displayed/assumed complete hostility.'
segments.s360-g22: ابا is used as a verb 'to refuse / be reluctant' (he refused). تو is the object 'you'. چنو 'چنین/چنو' = 'thus/so' links to کرد 'he did/made' (کرد is the simple past of کردن used here as a causative/making verb). یارد منی is a small predicative phrase with یارد 'helper/companion', من 'me' and the archaic copula ی (is) — literal 'is my helper' / 'made me his helper'. Taken together: 'he refused you; thus he made me his helper.'
segments.s360-g23: چو = 'when'. ارجاسپ is the subject (proper name). بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (3rd-person singular). گفتار دیو = 'the speech/words of the demon' (گفتار 'speech' governed by the genitive/juxtaposed possessor دیو 'demon').
segments.s360-g24: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'he descended / came down' (فرود 'descent' + آمد 'came'). از introduces the source 'from'. گاه here functions as 'court/seat' or 'place/occasion' (in classical usage can mean a place/bench of authority), and گیهان خدیو together form a title 'the khedive (خدیو) of the world (گیهان)' — so 'from the court/place of the world's khedive.'
segments.s360-g49: گرفتند is past 3rd-person-plural 'they took/accepted'. ازو is the enclitic/independent prepositional phrase 'from him'. سربسر is an adverbial intensifier 'entirely/wholly' that qualifies the following noun. دین اوی is a noun + possessive (اوی = 'his'), so the phrase means 'his religion' and the whole clause: 'they took from him his entire religion.'
segments.s360-g50: This is a verbal clause of result: جهان شد پر از ... = 'the world became full of ...'. شد is the past/preterite 'became'. پر + از marks 'full of', and راه و آیین اوی is a coordinated noun phrase 'his ways and customs' with اوی as the possessive. Word order is Subject - (aux)Verb - predicate (typical in Persian poetry).
segments.s360-g51: نشست here is the past intransitive 'he sat/settled'. او is the subject 'he'. به ایران is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/into Iran' (به = 'towards/into'). به پیغمبری is a second complement using به + noun: 'in the role of / as a prophet.' The clause strings location and role after the verb in typical Persian order.
segments.s360-g52: به کاری = 'with respect to a work/task' (به as 'in/with'). کاری functions as 'a work/task' (here treated as an object noun). چنان is an intensifier 'such/so', and یافه is a past participle 'found/obtained' modifying the noun phrase (literally 'having found such a task'). و سرسری coordinates an adjectival comment 'and cursory/trivial', so the whole phrase says he came with/into such a trivial employment.
segments.s360-g53: باید is a modal 'must/ought', taking the infinitive نوشتن as its complement: باید نوشتن 'must write'. یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone' (or 'one'), نامه is the object 'a letter', and کنون is the adverb 'now' — together: 'someone must now write a letter.'
segments.s360-g54: سوی آن = 'to/unto that one' (سوی = toward/to + آن = that). زده is a past participle 'struck/stricken' modifying سر 'head' (زده سر can idiomatically indicate someone struck or disgraced). ز فرمان برون is a compact phrase 'out of command' (ز = 'from', فرمان = 'command/office', برون = 'out'), describing that person's removal from authority. The word order is compact and somewhat elliptical, typical of epic diction.
segments.s360-g55: ببایدش is a contracted construction of باید + ش (clitic 'to him/her') meaning 'it must (be) to him' or 'one ought (to give) him'. دادن is the infinitive 'to give' serving as the complement of باید. بسی quantifies خواسته: 'many/much desired' — خواسته is a past-participle noun meaning 'that which is desired' or 'wishes'. The sequence expresses necessity to give him many desired things.
segments.s360-g56: که here functions causally 'for/because'. نیکو بود = 'it was good' (بود as past copula). داده is the past participle 'given' and ناخواسته qualifies it 'without being asked / unintentionally' — together: 'for it was good to give even unasked.' The clause explains the reason for the previous injunction.

sentences.s360-s33: Let us go into Iran in pursuit of his affairs; let us not fear his injury and battle.
